---
title: "Gamma, Exponential, and Beta"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article,
# matched to the rest of the Probability Foundations track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CURVE  = "#2b3a67"     # the density curve / data
CURVE2 = "#c1121f"     # comparison curve / fitted line
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / the mean
GREY   = "#6c757d"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fifth stop in the **distribution catalog**
> of the Probability Foundations track. It assumes [PROB-4 *Continuous Random Variables: the
> Probability Density Function*] for what a density is and how
> $P(a\le X\le b)=\int_a^b f$ recovers probabilities; [PROB-5 *Expectation*] and
> [PROB-6 *Variance*] for computing a mean and variance from a density; and
> [PROB-12 *The Poisson Distribution*] for the rare-event count whose *gaps in time* turn out
> to be the exponential we build here. The one tool we reach for above the Calculus-I floor
> — **integration by parts** and a single recursive integral — is a Calc-1 technique that we
> state and use openly. It **sets up** [PROB-15 *Chi-Square, Student's t, and F*], where the
> chi-square is revealed as a special gamma, and [PROB-16 *The Exponential Family*], where
> all three of these distributions reappear as members of one form. Downstream it is consumed
> by Statistical Inference's conjugate-Bayes machinery ([INFR-E4]), which uses the gamma and
> the beta as priors.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Three distributions, one parent. The **exponential** models the waiting time until a single
random event; the **gamma** models the waiting time until the $\alpha$-th such event; and the
**beta** models a random *proportion* trapped in $[0,1]$. This article derives all three from
the ground up and shows they are one family. It first builds the **gamma function**
$\Gamma(\alpha)=\int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt$ — the continuous factorial — and proves
its recurrence $\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\Gamma(\alpha-1)$ in full, because that single
integral is the normalizing constant all three distributions need. It then derives the
exponential as the **memoryless** waiting time of a Poisson process (memorylessness proved,
not asserted), builds the gamma as a **sum of independent exponentials**, and constructs the
beta from a **ratio of gammas** — deriving its mean and variance along the way. The single
claim the article defends is that these are not three unrelated formula sheets but **one
mechanism seen from three angles**: the gamma function ties them, and the beta–gamma identity
makes the family literally close on itself. We close on the honest boundary — the exponential's
own memorylessness is exactly why it is the *wrong* model for anything that ages or wears out.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **gamma function** $\Gamma(\alpha)=\int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt$ is the
continuous version of the factorial ($\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$), and it is the normalizing constant
behind all three distributions of this article. The **exponential**$(\lambda)$ is the waiting
time until the first event of a rate-$\lambda$ Poisson process; it is the *unique* continuous
distribution with **no memory** — having waited an hour tells you nothing about the wait
remaining. The **gamma**$(\alpha,\beta)$ is the waiting time until the $\alpha$-th event,
equivalently the sum of $\alpha$ independent exponentials. The **beta**$(\alpha,\beta)$ lives
on $[0,1]$ and models a random proportion; it is built as a normalized ratio of two gammas,
which is exactly why it is the natural distribution for an unknown probability. One parent —
the gamma function — three children.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The gamma function's recurrence is proved in
@sec-gamma-function; memorylessness in @sec-memoryless; the gamma-as-sum-of-exponentials in
@sec-gamma-dist; and the beta and its beta–gamma tie in @sec-beta. If a step ever feels
arbitrary, return to the Core: we are describing one waiting-and-proportion mechanism, and
every formula is a fingerprint of it.

# Why we are here: the gap between the counts {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view the whole way through: **once you can count random events, how do
you describe the time *between* them and the *fraction* of them that come out one way?** The
distribution catalog so far answered "how many?" — Bernoulli and binomial counted successes
([PROB-11]), Poisson counted rare events in a window ([PROB-12]). But counting leaves two
natural questions unanswered, and those two questions are this article.

## The problem the exponential was invented to solve

[PROB-12] modeled the **number** of rare events in a fixed interval — calls to a switchboard
in an hour, decays of a radioactive sample in a second, customers arriving in a day. But an
operator does not experience "$3.2$ calls per hour" as a number; they experience **gaps**:
the call comes, then a stretch of quiet, then another call. The natural question is not "how
many this hour?" but "**how long until the next one?**" That waiting time is a *continuous*
random variable on $[0,\infty)$, and the distribution that governs it is the exponential.

The historical thread runs straight through reliability and physics. Radioactive decay was
the first place the question bit hard: Rutherford and Geiger, counting alpha particles around
1908, found the *counts* in fixed intervals were Poisson — and therefore the *waiting times
between* particles had to be exponential, because the two are the same process seen as "how
many" versus "how long." The same pair governs queues (Erlang's telephone-traffic work,
1909), component lifetimes, and the inter-arrival times of essentially any process where
events happen independently at a constant average rate.

## The problem the gamma and beta were invented to solve

Two refinements force themselves immediately. First, you rarely care about just the *next*
event — you care about the wait until the *$\alpha$-th*: until the third server fails, until
the tenth customer arrives, until enough decays have accumulated to register. Add up $\alpha$
independent exponential gaps and you get the **gamma**. The gamma is the exponential's
"several waits later" generalization, and (we will see) it is also what you get when the
"number of waits" $\alpha$ is allowed to be a *fraction*, which is where the gamma function
comes in.

Second, a completely different need: many quantities are not times at all but **proportions**
— the fraction of voters who approve, the conversion rate of a website, the reliability of a
component as a number in $[0,1]$. A proportion is *bounded*; the normal ([PROB-13]) puts
probability outside $[0,1]$ and is the wrong shape, while the gamma lives on $[0,\infty)$ and
is also wrong. We need a distribution *on the unit interval*. The **beta** is that
distribution — and, remarkably, it is built out of gammas, so the same machinery serves both
needs. By the end you will see the three as one family, joined at the gamma function.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the relationship between the Poisson and the exponential?"* They are the **same
process** described two ways. A Poisson process has events occurring independently at a
constant average rate $\lambda$; the Poisson distribution ([PROB-12]) counts **how many**
events land in a fixed window, while the exponential measures **how long** you wait between
consecutive events. "Counts are Poisson" and "gaps are exponential" are logically equivalent
statements about the same underlying randomness — we prove that equivalence in
@sec-exp-from-poisson, rather than leave it as a slogan. If an interviewer asks you to derive
one from the other, that derivation is the move they want.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Three pictures, no symbols manipulated yet.

**The exponential is a constant-hazard wait.** Imagine standing at a bus stop where, in *any*
given minute, the bus has the same small chance of arriving, regardless of how long you have
already waited. The buses do not "build up." This constant per-instant chance is the defining
feature, and it forces the waiting-time density to *decay at a constant proportional rate* —
which is exactly what $e^{-\lambda x}$ does. The longer waits are rarer, and they get rarer by
the same factor for each additional unit of waiting. The whole shape is a single downhill
slide from a peak at zero.

**The gamma is a pile of exponential waits.** If one wait is exponential, then the time until
the third event is the sum of three independent exponential gaps. Summing pushes the bulk away
from zero (it is unlikely that *all three* gaps are tiny) and pulls a right tail (any one gap
can be long). So the gamma starts at zero, rises to a hump, and trails off to the right — and
the more events you wait for, the more the hump moves right and the more symmetric it looks
(a preview of the Central Limit Theorem, owned by [INFR-A2]).

**The beta is a tug-of-war on $[0,1]$.** Picture two opposing pulls on a point in the unit
interval: a parameter $\alpha$ pulling toward $1$ and a parameter $\beta$ pulling toward $0$.
When $\alpha=\beta$ the density is symmetric about $1/2$; raise $\alpha$ and the mass slides
right, raise $\beta$ and it slides left. With $\alpha=\beta=1$ the two pulls cancel into a
flat line — the uniform distribution — which is why the beta is the natural way to express
"a probability I am unsure about." @fig-gallery shows all three families.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gallery
#| fig-cap: "The three families this article builds. **Left:** exponentials — a downhill slide from a peak at zero, steeper for larger rate $\\lambda$. **Middle:** gammas with fixed scale — as the shape $\\alpha$ grows, the hump moves right and rounds out (a sum of more waits). **Right:** betas on $[0,1]$ — symmetric when $\\alpha=\\beta$, flat (uniform) when $\\alpha=\\beta=1$, and pushed toward an end when the parameters are unequal."
from scipy.stats import expon, gamma as gamma_d, beta as beta_d

fig, (axL, axM, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.3))

x = np.linspace(0, 6, 400)
for lam, c in [(0.6, ACCENT), (1.0, CURVE), (2.0, CURVE2)]:
    axL.plot(x, lam*np.exp(-lam*x), color=c, label=fr"$\lambda={lam}$")
axL.set_title("Exponential"); axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

xg = np.linspace(0, 14, 400)
for a, c in [(1, ACCENT), (2, CURVE), (5, CURVE2)]:
    axM.plot(xg, gamma_d.pdf(xg, a, scale=1.5), color=c, label=fr"$\alpha={a},\ \beta=1.5$")
axM.set_title(r"Gamma (scale $\beta=1.5$)"); axM.set_xlabel("$x$")
axM.legend(fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)

xb = np.linspace(0, 1, 400)
for (a, b), c in [((1, 1), GREY), ((2, 2), CURVE), ((5, 2), CURVE2), ((2, 5), ACCENT)]:
    axR.plot(xb, beta_d.pdf(xb, a, b), color=c, label=fr"$\alpha={a},\ \beta={b}$")
axR.set_title("Beta on $[0,1]$"); axR.set_xlabel("$x$")
axR.legend(fontsize=8.0, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Formal development {#sec-formal}

## The gamma function: a continuous factorial {#sec-gamma-function}

Before any distribution, the constant they all share. The factorial $n!=n\cdot(n-1)\cdots 1$
is defined only for non-negative integers. We are about to need a version that works for any
positive real number $\alpha$ — because, for instance, "wait for $2.5$ events" will turn out
to make sense. The gamma function is that extension.

::: {#def-gamma-function}
**Definition (gamma function).** For any real $\alpha>0$,
$$
\Gamma(\alpha)=\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt.
$$ {#eq-gamma-function}
:::

Every symbol earns its keep: $t$ is the dummy integration variable, $t^{\alpha-1}$ is a power
that grows with $t$, and $e^{-t}$ is the decay that drags the product back to zero so the area
is finite. The integral converges for every $\alpha>0$: near $t=0$ the integrand behaves like
$t^{\alpha-1}$, which is integrable there exactly when $\alpha>0$ (the Calc-1 fact that
$\int_0^1 t^{p}\,dt$ is finite iff $p>-1$), and as $t\to\infty$ the $e^{-t}$ decay beats any
power $t^{\alpha-1}$, so the tail contributes a finite amount. That is why the definition
restricts to $\alpha>0$.

The one property we will use everywhere is its recurrence, which is what makes it "factorial-
like." We prove it by integration by parts — a Calc-1 technique, stated and used openly.

::: {#thm-gamma-recurrence}
**Theorem (the gamma recurrence).** For every $\alpha>1$,
$$
\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\,\Gamma(\alpha-1).
$$ {#eq-gamma-recurrence}
Consequently, for every positive integer $n$, $\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Start from the definition and apply **integration by parts**, $\int u\,dv=uv-\int v\,du$. The
choice that lowers the power is
$$
u=t^{\alpha-1},\qquad dv=e^{-t}\,dt,
$$
so that $du=(\alpha-1)t^{\alpha-2}\,dt$ (the Calc-1 power rule) and $v=-e^{-t}$ (since
$\frac{d}{dt}(-e^{-t})=e^{-t}$). Then
$$
\Gamma(\alpha)=\int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt
=\Bigl[\,\underbrace{t^{\alpha-1}\cdot(-e^{-t})}_{uv}\,\Bigr]_0^\infty
-\int_0^\infty (-e^{-t})\,(\alpha-1)t^{\alpha-2}\,dt.
$$
Examine the boundary term $\bigl[-t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\bigr]_0^\infty$. At the upper end, as
$t\to\infty$, the exponential $e^{-t}$ crushes the power $t^{\alpha-1}$ to zero (the
exponential decays faster than any power grows), so the term is $0$ there. At the lower end
$t=0$, the factor $t^{\alpha-1}\to 0$ because $\alpha-1>0$ (this is exactly where we use
$\alpha>1$), so the term is $0$ there too. The boundary term vanishes entirely.

What remains is
$$
\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-2}e^{-t}\,dt
=(\alpha-1)\int_0^\infty t^{(\alpha-1)-1}e^{-t}\,dt
=(\alpha-1)\,\Gamma(\alpha-1),
$$
where the last equality is just the definition @eq-gamma-function read at the argument
$\alpha-1$. That proves @eq-gamma-recurrence.

For the integer fact, first compute the base case directly:
$$
\Gamma(1)=\int_0^\infty t^{0}e^{-t}\,dt=\int_0^\infty e^{-t}\,dt
=\bigl[-e^{-t}\bigr]_0^\infty=0-(-1)=1.
$$
Now apply the recurrence repeatedly: $\Gamma(2)=1\cdot\Gamma(1)=1=1!$,
$\Gamma(3)=2\cdot\Gamma(2)=2=2!$, and in general
$\Gamma(n)=(n-1)\Gamma(n-1)=(n-1)(n-2)\cdots 1\cdot\Gamma(1)=(n-1)!$. So $\Gamma$ interpolates
the factorial through every positive real, agreeing with $(n-1)!$ at the integers.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

One more value will surface when the gamma meets the normal in [PROB-15], and we record it now
so the chain stays explicit. The half-integer value $\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt{\pi}$ is *not*
proved here: it reduces to the Gaussian integral $\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-x^2/2}\,dx=\sqrt{2\pi}$,
which is **built in full in [PROB-13] @sec-gaussian-integral**. We flag it as that imported
ingredient rather than re-derive the polar-coordinates trick — the only above-floor object we
borrow rather than build, and it is built elsewhere in this same track.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why $\alpha-1$ in the exponent of the integrand, not $\alpha$? It looks like an off-by-one
trap."* It is a deliberate convention that makes the recurrence and the integer case land on
$\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$ rather than $n!$. The "$-1$" is the price of having
$\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\Gamma(\alpha-1)$ come out clean. It does cause a perennial
off-by-one headache when you connect $\Gamma$ to the factorial — the thing to memorize is
$\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$, *not* $\Gamma(n)=n!$. Stating that you know the shift exists, and which
way it goes, is exactly what the question is checking.
:::

## The exponential distribution {#sec-exponential}

::: {#def-exponential}
**Definition (exponential distribution).** A continuous random variable $X$ is
**exponentially distributed** with **rate** $\lambda>0$, written $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$,
if its density is
$$
f(x)=\lambda e^{-\lambda x},\qquad x\ge 0,
$$ {#eq-exp-density}
and $f(x)=0$ for $x<0$.
:::

The rate $\lambda$ has units of "events per unit time"; its reciprocal $1/\lambda$ is the
mean wait, as we are about to prove. First, two sanity checks done in full.

**It is a valid density.** A density must integrate to $1$ over its support ([PROB-4]):
$$
\int_0^\infty \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\lambda\Bigl[-\tfrac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda x}\Bigr]_0^\infty
=\lambda\cdot\tfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl(0-(-1)\bigr)=1,
$$
using $\frac{d}{dx}\bigl(-\tfrac1\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\bigr)=e^{-\lambda x}$ and
$e^{-\lambda x}\to 0$ as $x\to\infty$. Good.

**Its CDF.** For $x\ge 0$,
$$
F(x)=P(X\le x)=\int_0^x \lambda e^{-\lambda u}\,du
=\bigl[-e^{-\lambda u}\bigr]_0^x=1-e^{-\lambda x}.
$$ {#eq-exp-cdf}
The complementary form $P(X>x)=e^{-\lambda x}$ — the **survival function** — is the one we
will lean on for memorylessness; it is the probability the wait exceeds $x$.

### Mean and variance {#sec-exp-moments}

::: {#thm-exp-moments}
**Theorem (moments of the exponential).** If $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ then
$$
E[X]=\frac{1}{\lambda},\qquad \operatorname{Var}(X)=\frac{1}{\lambda^2}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
**The mean.** By the definition of expectation for a continuous variable ([PROB-5]),
$$
E[X]=\int_0^\infty x\,\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx.
$$
Integrate by parts with $u=x$, $dv=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx$, so $du=dx$ and
$v=-e^{-\lambda x}$:
$$
E[X]=\bigl[\,x\cdot(-e^{-\lambda x})\,\bigr]_0^\infty-\int_0^\infty(-e^{-\lambda x})\,dx
=\bigl[-x e^{-\lambda x}\bigr]_0^\infty+\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda x}\,dx.
$$
The boundary term is $0$ at both ends: at $x=0$ the factor $x$ is zero, and as $x\to\infty$
the exponential beats the linear $x$. The remaining integral is
$\int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda x}\,dx=\bigl[-\tfrac1\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\bigr]_0^\infty=\tfrac1\lambda$.
So $E[X]=1/\lambda$ — the average wait is the reciprocal of the rate, exactly as the units
demand.

**The variance.** Use the computational identity
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2$ ([PROB-6]). We need $E[X^2]$:
$$
E[X^2]=\int_0^\infty x^2\,\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx.
$$
Integrate by parts with $u=x^2$, $dv=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx$, giving $du=2x\,dx$ and
$v=-e^{-\lambda x}$:
$$
E[X^2]=\bigl[-x^2 e^{-\lambda x}\bigr]_0^\infty+\int_0^\infty 2x\,e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=0+\frac{2}{\lambda}\int_0^\infty x\,\lambda e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\frac{2}{\lambda}\cdot E[X]=\frac{2}{\lambda}\cdot\frac1\lambda=\frac{2}{\lambda^2}.
$$
The boundary term vanishes by the same reasoning as before, and we recognized the leftover
integral as $\tfrac{2}{\lambda}$ times the mean integral we already evaluated. Therefore
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2=\frac{2}{\lambda^2}-\Bigl(\frac1\lambda\Bigr)^2
=\frac{2}{\lambda^2}-\frac{1}{\lambda^2}=\frac{1}{\lambda^2}.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

Notice $\operatorname{Var}(X)=(E[X])^2$: the standard deviation equals the mean. An
exponential wait is as variable as it is long — a high-variance, right-skewed object, nothing
like the tidy normal.

### Memorylessness — the property that defines it {#sec-memoryless}

Here is the structural fact that singles the exponential out, and we prove it both directions
rather than assert it.

::: {#thm-memoryless}
**Theorem (memorylessness).** $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ satisfies, for all $s,t\ge 0$,
$$
P(X>s+t\mid X>s)=P(X>t).
$$ {#eq-memoryless}
Conversely, the exponential is the *only* continuous distribution on $[0,\infty)$ with this
property.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Forward direction.** By the definition of conditional probability ([PROB-1]),
$$
P(X>s+t\mid X>s)=\frac{P(\{X>s+t\}\cap\{X>s\})}{P(X>s)}.
$$
Since $s+t\ge s$, the event $\{X>s+t\}$ is contained in $\{X>s\}$, so their intersection is
just $\{X>s+t\}$. Using the survival function $P(X>x)=e^{-\lambda x}$ from @eq-exp-cdf,
$$
P(X>s+t\mid X>s)=\frac{P(X>s+t)}{P(X>s)}=\frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}}
=e^{-\lambda(s+t)+\lambda s}=e^{-\lambda t}=P(X>t).
$$
The $e^{-\lambda s}$ cancels completely. Having already waited $s$ leaves *no trace*: the
remaining wait has the same distribution as a fresh start. That is what "memoryless" means.

**Converse direction.** Suppose a continuous random variable $X$ on $[0,\infty)$ satisfies
@eq-memoryless. Write its survival function $G(x)=P(X>x)$. The memoryless condition, after
multiplying through by $P(X>s)=G(s)$, says
$$
P(X>s+t)=P(X>s)\,P(X>t),\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad G(s+t)=G(s)\,G(t)
$$
for all $s,t\ge 0$. This is the **Cauchy functional equation for products**. We solve it. Let
$g(x)=\ln G(x)$, valid where $G(x)>0$ (and $G$ is positive on $[0,\infty)$, else the variable
would be bounded and could not satisfy the relation for all $t$). Taking logs turns the
product into a sum:
$$
g(s+t)=g(s)+g(t).
$$
This is *additive*. For a function that is monotone (here $G$ is non-increasing, so $g$ is
too) the only solutions of $g(s+t)=g(s)+g(t)$ are linear, $g(x)=cx$ for some constant $c$. We
verify and pin $c$: from additivity, $g(nx)=ng(x)$ for integer $n$ and
$g(x)=g(\tfrac{x}{m}\cdot m)=m\,g(\tfrac{x}{m})$, so $g(\tfrac{p}{q}x)=\tfrac{p}{q}g(x)$ for
every rational $p/q$; monotonicity then forces the same linear relation at every real
multiple, giving $g(x)=x\,g(1)=cx$ with $c=g(1)$. Since $G$ is a survival function it is
non-increasing and $G(x)\le 1$, so $g(x)=\ln G(x)\le 0$, forcing $c\le 0$; write $c=-\lambda$
with $\lambda\ge 0$. Then
$$
G(x)=e^{g(x)}=e^{-\lambda x}.
$$
A non-degenerate distribution needs $\lambda>0$. But $G(x)=e^{-\lambda x}$ is exactly the
exponential survival function @eq-exp-cdf, whose density is $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$. So the
exponential is the unique continuous memoryless distribution on $[0,\infty)$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What does 'memoryless' actually mean, and why is the exponential the only continuous
distribution that has it?"* Memoryless means the past does not change the future: given that
you have already waited $s$, the distribution of the *remaining* wait is identical to the
distribution of the *whole* wait from scratch — formally @eq-memoryless. The reason the
exponential is the unique continuous case is the functional equation $G(s+t)=G(s)G(t)$, whose
only monotone solution is $G(x)=e^{-\lambda x}$ (proved just above). The discrete analogue is
the geometric distribution ([PROB-11]), memoryless on the integers for the same algebraic
reason. Interviewers love this because it connects a *property* (no aging) to a *form* (the
exponential), and the bridge is a clean functional-equation argument.
:::

### The exponential *is* the Poisson gap {#sec-exp-from-poisson}

We promised in @sec-why that "Poisson counts" and "exponential gaps" are the same process. We
prove it now, building directly on [PROB-12].

::: {#thm-poisson-exp}
**Theorem (waiting times in a Poisson process).** In a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ —
where the number of events in any interval of length $x$ is $\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda x)$ —
the waiting time $T$ until the first event is $\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
The key observation: the waiting time $T$ exceeds $x$ **if and only if** *zero* events occur
in the interval $(0,x]$. (If the first event had arrived by time $x$, the wait would be at
most $x$; conversely if no event has arrived by $x$, the wait exceeds $x$.) So
$$
P(T>x)=P\bigl(\text{no events in }(0,x]\bigr).
$$
The number of events in an interval of length $x$ is $N\sim\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda x)$, whose
pmf ([PROB-12]) is $P(N=k)=\dfrac{(\lambda x)^k e^{-\lambda x}}{k!}$. Set $k=0$:
$$
P(N=0)=\frac{(\lambda x)^0 e^{-\lambda x}}{0!}=e^{-\lambda x}.
$$
Therefore $P(T>x)=e^{-\lambda x}$, so $P(T\le x)=1-e^{-\lambda x}$, which is exactly the
exponential CDF @eq-exp-cdf. Differentiating, the density is
$\frac{d}{dx}(1-e^{-\lambda x})=\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$. Hence $T\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the bridge: the *count* in a window is Poisson, the *gap* between events is
exponential, and they share the single rate $\lambda$. Memorylessness is the same coin's
other face — a Poisson process has no memory (events in disjoint intervals are independent),
so the wait for the next one cannot depend on how long you have already waited.

## The gamma distribution {#sec-gamma-dist}

If one gap is exponential, the time until the $\alpha$-th event is a *sum* of gaps. That sum
is the gamma. We give the definition, then derive it as a sum.

::: {#def-gamma-dist}
**Definition (gamma distribution).** A continuous random variable $X$ has the **gamma
distribution** with **shape** $\alpha>0$ and **rate** $\lambda>0$, written
$X\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\lambda)$, if its density is
$$
f(x)=\frac{\lambda^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\,x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda x},\qquad x\ge 0,
$$ {#eq-gamma-density}
and $0$ for $x<0$. (Some texts use the **scale** $\beta=1/\lambda$ instead; then
$f(x)=\frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)\beta^\alpha}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-x/\beta}$. We use the rate
throughout, to match the exponential's $\lambda$; we flag the scale form because [PROB-15] and
much software default to it.)
:::

Notice the pieces: $x^{\alpha-1}$ is the rising power, $e^{-\lambda x}$ is the decay, and
$\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}$ is the normalizing constant — and *there is the gamma
function*, doing the job we built it for. When $\alpha=1$ the power $x^{\alpha-1}=x^0=1$ and
$\Gamma(1)=1$, so @eq-gamma-density collapses to $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$: the exponential is
$\mathrm{Gamma}(1,\lambda)$, exactly as the "wait for one event" story demands.

**It integrates to $1$.** This is where the gamma function pays for itself. Substitute
$t=\lambda x$, so $x=t/\lambda$ and $dx=dt/\lambda$:
$$
\int_0^\infty \frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^\infty\Bigl(\frac{t}{\lambda}\Bigr)^{\alpha-1}
   e^{-t}\,\frac{dt}{\lambda}
=\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda^{\alpha-1}}\cdot\frac{1}{\lambda}
   \int_0^\infty t^{\alpha-1}e^{-t}\,dt.
$$
The powers of $\lambda$ collect to $\lambda^\alpha/\lambda^{\alpha-1}/\lambda=\lambda^0=1$, and
the remaining integral is precisely $\Gamma(\alpha)$ by @eq-gamma-function. So the whole thing
is $\frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\cdot\Gamma(\alpha)=1$. The normalizing constant is *forced* to be
$\lambda^\alpha/\Gamma(\alpha)$; there was no freedom in it.

### Mean, variance, and the sum-of-exponentials structure {#sec-gamma-moments}

::: {#thm-gamma-moments}
**Theorem (moments of the gamma).** If $X\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\lambda)$ then
$$
E[X]=\frac{\alpha}{\lambda},\qquad \operatorname{Var}(X)=\frac{\alpha}{\lambda^2}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We compute $E[X^r]$ for a general power $r$ in one stroke, because both moments fall out and
the gamma function does the work. By [PROB-5],
$$
E[X^r]=\int_0^\infty x^r\,\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}x^{\alpha-1}e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\int_0^\infty x^{(\alpha+r)-1}e^{-\lambda x}\,dx.
$$
The remaining integral is the *unnormalized* gamma density with shape $\alpha+r$. Substitute
$t=\lambda x$ exactly as in the normalization check above:
$$
\int_0^\infty x^{(\alpha+r)-1}e^{-\lambda x}\,dx
=\frac{1}{\lambda^{\alpha+r}}\int_0^\infty t^{(\alpha+r)-1}e^{-t}\,dt
=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+r)}{\lambda^{\alpha+r}}.
$$
Therefore
$$
E[X^r]=\frac{\lambda^\alpha}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\cdot\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+r)}{\lambda^{\alpha+r}}
=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+r)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\lambda^{r}}.
$$ {#eq-gamma-moment}
Now read off the two moments using the recurrence @eq-gamma-recurrence. For $r=1$,
$\Gamma(\alpha+1)=\alpha\,\Gamma(\alpha)$, so
$$
E[X]=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+1)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\lambda}=\frac{\alpha\,\Gamma(\alpha)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\lambda}=\frac{\alpha}{\lambda}.
$$
For $r=2$, apply the recurrence twice:
$\Gamma(\alpha+2)=(\alpha+1)\Gamma(\alpha+1)=(\alpha+1)\alpha\,\Gamma(\alpha)$, so
$$
E[X^2]=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+2)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\lambda^2}=\frac{(\alpha+1)\alpha}{\lambda^2}.
$$
Then
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2
=\frac{(\alpha+1)\alpha}{\lambda^2}-\frac{\alpha^2}{\lambda^2}
=\frac{\alpha^2+\alpha-\alpha^2}{\lambda^2}=\frac{\alpha}{\lambda^2}.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

These match the "sum of $\alpha$ exponentials" picture exactly. If $X=\sum_{i=1}^\alpha Y_i$
with $Y_i\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ independent (for integer $\alpha$), then linearity of
expectation ([PROB-5]) gives $E[X]=\alpha\cdot\frac1\lambda=\frac\alpha\lambda$, and
independence makes variances add ([PROB-8]),
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=\alpha\cdot\frac1{\lambda^2}=\frac\alpha{\lambda^2}$. The moments we got
from the gamma function and the moments we get from the sum-of-exponentials picture **agree**,
which is the consistency we should demand. We now prove that the sum really *is* gamma, not
just that the first two moments match.

::: {#thm-gamma-sum}
**Theorem (gamma is a sum of exponentials).** If $Y_1,\dots,Y_n$ are independent
$\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ variables, then $S_n=Y_1+\cdots+Y_n\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(n,\lambda)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We induct on $n$, using the **convolution** formula for the density of a sum of independent
continuous variables ([PROB-8]): if $S=A+B$ with $A,B$ independent, then
$f_S(s)=\int f_A(t)f_B(s-t)\,dt$ over the values where both densities are nonzero.

*Base case* $n=1$: $S_1=Y_1\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)=\mathrm{Gamma}(1,\lambda)$, already noted.

*Inductive step.* Assume $S_{n-1}\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(n-1,\lambda)$, with density
$f_{n-1}(t)=\frac{\lambda^{n-1}}{\Gamma(n-1)}t^{n-2}e^{-\lambda t}$ for $t\ge 0$. Then
$S_n=S_{n-1}+Y_n$, and both summands are supported on $[0,\infty)$, so for $s\ge 0$ the
convolution runs over $0\le t\le s$ (we need $t\ge 0$ for $S_{n-1}$ and $s-t\ge 0$ for $Y_n$):
$$
f_n(s)=\int_0^s f_{n-1}(t)\,\lambda e^{-\lambda(s-t)}\,dt
=\int_0^s \frac{\lambda^{n-1}}{\Gamma(n-1)}t^{n-2}e^{-\lambda t}\cdot\lambda e^{-\lambda(s-t)}\,dt.
$$
Pull out constants and combine the exponentials, $e^{-\lambda t}e^{-\lambda(s-t)}=e^{-\lambda s}$
(the $t$ cancels):
$$
f_n(s)=\frac{\lambda^{n}}{\Gamma(n-1)}\,e^{-\lambda s}\int_0^s t^{n-2}\,dt.
$$
The integral is elementary, $\int_0^s t^{n-2}\,dt=\dfrac{s^{n-1}}{n-1}$. And $\Gamma(n-1)$ with
the extra factor $\tfrac{1}{n-1}$ rebuilds the next factorial: $(n-1)\Gamma(n-1)=\Gamma(n)$ by
the recurrence @eq-gamma-recurrence, so $\dfrac{1}{(n-1)\Gamma(n-1)}=\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(n)}$.
Thus
$$
f_n(s)=\frac{\lambda^{n}}{\Gamma(n-1)}\,e^{-\lambda s}\cdot\frac{s^{n-1}}{n-1}
=\frac{\lambda^{n}}{\Gamma(n)}\,s^{n-1}e^{-\lambda s},
$$
which is exactly the $\mathrm{Gamma}(n,\lambda)$ density @eq-gamma-density. By induction the
claim holds for all $n$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

So the gamma is, for integer shape, literally "wait for $n$ events." The gamma function lets
us *also* read @eq-gamma-density for non-integer $\alpha$ — "wait for $2.5$ events" — which
has no event-counting story but is a perfectly good, often-needed distribution (it is what we
fit to skewed positive data in @sec-rainfall). @fig-gamma-build shows the sum building up.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gamma-build
#| fig-cap: "The gamma as a pile of exponential waits. Each panel histograms the sum $S_n=Y_1+\\cdots+Y_n$ of $n$ independent $\\mathrm{Exp}(1)$ gaps (so $\\lambda=1$) and overlays the predicted $\\mathrm{Gamma}(n,1)$ density (red). At $n=1$ it is the bare exponential; as $n$ grows the hump marches right to $E[S_n]=n$ and rounds toward a bell — the Central Limit Theorem starting to bite."
from scipy.stats import gamma as gamma_d
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
N = 200_000
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 4, figsize=(10.0, 3.0), sharey=False)
for ax, n in zip(axes, (1, 2, 5, 12)):
    s = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=(N, n)).sum(axis=1)
    ax.hist(s, bins=120, density=True, color="#cfd8e8", edgecolor="none")
    xx = np.linspace(0, s.max(), 400)
    ax.plot(xx, gamma_d.pdf(xx, n, scale=1.0), color=CURVE2)
    ax.set_title(fr"$n={n}$  (mean ${n}$)", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("$S_n$")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The beta distribution {#sec-beta}

Now the change of scene: from times on $[0,\infty)$ to **proportions on $[0,1]$**. The beta is
the distribution of a random fraction, and we build it from gammas so the family closes on
itself.

::: {#def-beta}
**Definition (beta distribution).** A continuous random variable $X$ has the **beta
distribution** with parameters $\alpha>0,\beta>0$, written $X\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$,
if its density is
$$
f(x)=\frac{1}{B(\alpha,\beta)}\,x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1},\qquad 0\le x\le 1,
$$ {#eq-beta-density}
and $0$ elsewhere, where the normalizing constant is the **beta function**
$$
B(\alpha,\beta)=\int_0^1 x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}\,dx.
$$ {#eq-beta-function}
:::

The shape is a tug-of-war: $x^{\alpha-1}$ rewards values near $1$, $(1-x)^{\beta-1}$ rewards
values near $0$, and the exponents $\alpha,\beta$ set the strengths. With $\alpha=\beta=1$ both
powers are $1$ and $f(x)=1/B(1,1)$; we will see $B(1,1)=1$, so the density is the flat
**uniform** on $[0,1]$ — the "I have no idea what this probability is" distribution.

The constant $B(\alpha,\beta)$ is defined as an integral, which is unsatisfying. The beta–gamma
identity expresses it in closed form, and it is the structural link of the whole article.

::: {#thm-beta-gamma}
**Theorem (the beta–gamma identity).** For all $\alpha,\beta>0$,
$$
B(\alpha,\beta)=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}.
$$ {#eq-beta-gamma}
Moreover, if $U\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,1)$ and $V\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\beta,1)$ are
independent, then
$$
X=\frac{U}{U+V}\ \sim\ \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta),
$$
and $X$ is **independent** of the total $U+V$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We build the beta directly out of two gammas; the identity @eq-beta-gamma falls out of the
same computation, so nothing is asserted separately. Let $U\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,1)$ and
$V\sim\mathrm{Gamma}(\beta,1)$ be independent. Because they are independent, their joint
density is the product of the marginals ([PROB-8]), for $u,v>0$:
$$
f_{U,V}(u,v)=\frac{u^{\alpha-1}e^{-u}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\cdot\frac{v^{\beta-1}e^{-v}}{\Gamma(\beta)}
=\frac{u^{\alpha-1}v^{\beta-1}e^{-(u+v)}}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}.
$$
Change variables to the **fraction** and the **total**,
$$
x=\frac{u}{u+v},\qquad s=u+v,
$$
so that $u=xs$ and $v=s-u=s(1-x)$, with $x\in(0,1)$ and $s>0$. This is a two-variable change
of variables, so we need the Jacobian factor that rescales the area element — the
two-dimensional cousin of the Calc-1 substitution rule, used here at exactly the level
[PROB-4] introduces the single-variable version and flagged as such. The Jacobian matrix of
$(u,v)$ with respect to $(x,s)$ is
$$
\frac{\partial(u,v)}{\partial(x,s)}
=\begin{pmatrix}\partial u/\partial x & \partial u/\partial s\\[2pt]
\partial v/\partial x & \partial v/\partial s\end{pmatrix}
=\begin{pmatrix} s & x\\ -s & 1-x\end{pmatrix}.
$$
Its determinant is $s(1-x)-x(-s)=s(1-x)+sx=s$. The area element transforms as
$du\,dv=|s|\,dx\,ds=s\,dx\,ds$ (with $s>0$). Substitute $u=xs$, $v=s(1-x)$, and $u+v=s$ into
the joint density and multiply by the Jacobian $s$:
$$
f_{X,S}(x,s)=\frac{(xs)^{\alpha-1}\bigl(s(1-x)\bigr)^{\beta-1}e^{-s}}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}\cdot s.
$$
Collect the powers of $s$: from $(xs)^{\alpha-1}$ comes $s^{\alpha-1}$, from
$\bigl(s(1-x)\bigr)^{\beta-1}$ comes $s^{\beta-1}$, and the Jacobian adds one more $s^1$, for a
total of $s^{(\alpha-1)+(\beta-1)+1}=s^{\alpha+\beta-1}$. Separating the $x$-part from the
$s$-part:
$$
f_{X,S}(x,s)
=\underbrace{\frac{x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}}_{\text{depends on }x}
\ \cdot\ \underbrace{s^{\alpha+\beta-1}e^{-s}}_{\text{depends on }s}.
$$
The joint density **factors** into a function of $x$ alone times a function of $s$ alone — so
$X$ and $S$ are independent (this is the [PROB-8] criterion for independence), proving the
last claim. To extract the marginal density of $X$, integrate $s$ out over $(0,\infty)$:
$$
f_X(x)=\frac{x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}
\int_0^\infty s^{\alpha+\beta-1}e^{-s}\,ds
=\frac{x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta),
$$
where the remaining integral is $\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$ by @eq-gamma-function. So
$$
f_X(x)=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}\,x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}.
$$
This is precisely the beta density @eq-beta-density with normalizing constant
$\frac{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}$. Comparing with the definition
@eq-beta-function, we have simultaneously proved both that $X\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$
*and* that $B(\alpha,\beta)=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}$, since
the density must integrate to $1$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the punchline of the article. The beta is a **normalized competition between two
gammas** — $U/(U+V)$ — which is exactly why it is the natural model for a fraction: if $U$
counts "evidence for" and $V$ counts "evidence against," the proportion $U/(U+V)$ is beta. And
$B(1,1)=\frac{\Gamma(1)\Gamma(1)}{\Gamma(2)}=\frac{1\cdot1}{1}=1$, confirming
$\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ is the uniform.

### Mean and variance of the beta {#sec-beta-moments}

::: {#thm-beta-moments}
**Theorem (moments of the beta).** If $X\sim\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ then
$$
E[X]=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta},\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(X)=\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We compute the general moment $E[X^r]$ and the beta–gamma identity does the rest. By [PROB-5],
$$
E[X^r]=\int_0^1 x^r\,\frac{1}{B(\alpha,\beta)}x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}\,dx
=\frac{1}{B(\alpha,\beta)}\int_0^1 x^{(\alpha+r)-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1}\,dx.
$$
The remaining integral is, by the very definition @eq-beta-function, the beta function
$B(\alpha+r,\beta)$. So
$$
E[X^r]=\frac{B(\alpha+r,\beta)}{B(\alpha,\beta)}.
$$
Now turn each beta function into gammas via @eq-beta-gamma:
$$
E[X^r]
=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+r)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+r)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}
=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha+r)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+r)}.
$$ {#eq-beta-moment}
For the mean, $r=1$. Use $\Gamma(\alpha+1)=\alpha\Gamma(\alpha)$ and
$\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+1)=(\alpha+\beta)\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$ (the recurrence @eq-gamma-recurrence,
each applied once):
$$
E[X]=\frac{\alpha\Gamma(\alpha)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\,(\alpha+\beta)\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}
=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}.
$$
For $E[X^2]$, $r=2$. Now $\Gamma(\alpha+2)=(\alpha+1)\alpha\,\Gamma(\alpha)$ and
$\Gamma(\alpha+\beta+2)=(\alpha+\beta+1)(\alpha+\beta)\,\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$:
$$
E[X^2]=\frac{(\alpha+1)\alpha}{(\alpha+\beta+1)(\alpha+\beta)}.
$$
Then, writing $\mu=\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$ for the mean and combining over a common denominator,
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-\mu^2
=\frac{(\alpha+1)\alpha}{(\alpha+\beta+1)(\alpha+\beta)}-\frac{\alpha^2}{(\alpha+\beta)^2}.
$$
Put both terms over $(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)$. The first becomes
$\dfrac{(\alpha+1)\alpha(\alpha+\beta)}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}$ and the second
$\dfrac{\alpha^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}$. Subtract the numerators:
$$
(\alpha+1)\alpha(\alpha+\beta)-\alpha^2(\alpha+\beta+1)
=\alpha\bigl[(\alpha+1)(\alpha+\beta)-\alpha(\alpha+\beta+1)\bigr].
$$
Expand inside the bracket:
$(\alpha+1)(\alpha+\beta)=\alpha^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha+\beta$ and
$\alpha(\alpha+\beta+1)=\alpha^2+\alpha\beta+\alpha$; their difference is just $\beta$. So the
numerator is $\alpha\beta$, giving
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X)=\frac{\alpha\beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)}.\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The mean $\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$ reads beautifully through the $U/(U+V)$ construction: it is
the *fraction* of the total "mass" carried by $\alpha$. And the variance shrinks as
$\alpha+\beta$ grows with the ratio fixed — more total evidence, tighter the proportion —
which is exactly the behavior we want from a model of a probability we are learning about.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the beta the natural prior for an unknown probability, and what does conjugacy mean?"*
Two reasons, both visible now. First, **support**: a probability lives in $[0,1]$, and the beta
is the flexible distribution on exactly that interval — uniform when you are ignorant
($\alpha=\beta=1$), peaked when you are confident. Second, **conjugacy**: if your prior on a
coin's bias $p$ is $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ and you observe $k$ heads in $n$ flips
(a binomial likelihood $\propto p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$, [PROB-11]), then the posterior is
proportional to $p^{\alpha-1}(1-p)^{\beta-1}\cdot p^k(1-p)^{n-k}=p^{\alpha+k-1}(1-p)^{\beta+n-k-1}$
— *again a beta*, $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha+k,\ \beta+n-k)$. The prior and posterior live in the
same family, so updating is just "add the successes to $\alpha$, the failures to $\beta$." That
is conjugacy, and it is why the beta is the workhorse prior for proportions. The full Bayesian
machinery is owned by [INFR-E4]; the *distributional* reason it works is the algebra you just
saw — the powers of $p$ simply add.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the family on honest problems — including one where the exponential is the
*wrong* tool, so the boundary is visible and not a highlight reel.

## Example 1 — call-center waits and the no-show paradox {#sec-callcenter}

A support line receives calls as a Poisson process at rate $\lambda=`{python} "12"`$ calls per
hour, so the time between calls is $\mathrm{Exp}(12)$ with time measured in hours
(@thm-poisson-exp). Three questions, by hand from the CDF @eq-exp-cdf.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-callcenter
#| echo: false
lam = 12.0  # per hour
mean_min = 60.0 / lam
# Q1: P(wait > 10 minutes) = P(X > 1/6 hour)
p1 = np.exp(-lam * (10/60))
# Q2: median wait
median_hr = np.log(2) / lam
# Q3: memoryless -- already waited 8 min, P(wait > 10 more min)
p3 = np.exp(-lam * (10/60))
print(f"mean wait        = 1/lambda = {mean_min:.2f} minutes")
print(f"Q1 P(wait>10min) = e^(-12 * 1/6)        = {p1:.4f}")
print(f"Q2 median wait   = ln2/lambda            = {60*median_hr:.2f} minutes")
print(f"Q3 after 8 min already waited, P(>10 more) = {p3:.4f}  (same as Q1!)")
```

- **Mean wait.** $E[X]=1/\lambda=1/12$ hour $=`{python} f"{mean_min:.0f}"`$ minutes.
- **Chance of waiting more than 10 minutes** ($=1/6$ hour). The survival function gives
  $P(X>1/6)=e^{-12/6}=e^{-2}=`{python} f"{p1:.4f}"`$ — about $`{python} f"{100*p1:.0f}"`\%$.
- **The no-show "paradox."** You have already waited 8 minutes with no call. Probability you
  wait *at least 10 more*? By memorylessness (@thm-memoryless), the 8 minutes are forgotten:
  $P(X>8{+}10\mid X>8)=P(X>10)=`{python} f"{p3:.4f}"`$ — identical to a fresh start. The line
  does not "owe" you a call for having waited. This is the most counterintuitive consequence of
  the exponential, and it is exactly @eq-memoryless made concrete.

## Example 2 — reliability of a redundant system (the gamma) {#sec-reliability}

A satellite carries one active gyroscope plus **two cold spares**; when one fails, the next is
switched in. Each gyroscope's lifetime is $\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$ with mean
$1/\lambda=`{python} "2.0"`$ years, and failures are independent. The satellite functions until
*all three* have failed, so the total mission life is the sum of three independent exponential
lifetimes — a $\mathrm{Gamma}(3,\lambda)$ by @thm-gamma-sum. How likely is the mission to last
at least 5 years?

```{python}
#| label: tbl-reliability
#| echo: false
from math import factorial
from scipy.stats import gamma as gamma_d
lam = 0.5            # per year, mean life 2 yr
alpha = 3
mean_life = alpha / lam
# P(T >= 5): gamma survival
p_survive = gamma_d.sf(5.0, alpha, scale=1/lam)
# closed-form for integer shape: P(T>t)=e^{-lam t} sum_{k=0}^{alpha-1} (lam t)^k/k!
t = 5.0
closed = np.exp(-lam*t) * sum((lam*t)**k / factorial(k) for k in range(alpha))
print(f"mean mission life = alpha/lambda = {mean_life:.1f} years")
print(f"P(mission >= 5 yr) via gamma.sf       = {p_survive:.4f}")
print(f"P(mission >= 5 yr) via closed Poisson form = {closed:.4f}  (agree)")
```

The expected mission life is $E[T]=\alpha/\lambda=`{python} f"{mean_life:.0f}"`$ years
(@thm-gamma-moments). For the survival probability there is a clean closed form for *integer*
shape that ties straight back to the Poisson, and we use it as a cross-check rather than
trusting the library blindly. The mission outlives $t$ exactly when **fewer than $\alpha=3$**
failures have occurred by time $t$ in the rate-$\lambda$ failure process; the number of
failures in $[0,t]$ is $\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda t)$ ([PROB-12]), so
$$
P(T>t)=P\bigl(\text{at most }2\text{ failures by }t\bigr)
=\sum_{k=0}^{2}\frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}.
$$
With $\lambda=0.5$, $t=5$ (so $\lambda t=2.5$), this is
$e^{-2.5}\bigl(1+2.5+\tfrac{2.5^2}{2}\bigr)=`{python} f"{closed:.4f}"`$ — matching the gamma
computation. The redundancy is what buys the survival probability above: a single gyro
($\alpha=1$) would have $P(\text{life}>5)=e^{-2.5}\approx`{python} f"{np.exp(-2.5):.3f}"`$,
far worse. @fig-reliability shows the gamma survival curve sharpening with redundancy.

```{python}
#| label: fig-reliability
#| fig-cap: "Redundancy reshapes the lifetime. Each curve is the survival function $P(T>t)$ for a system that fails after $\\alpha$ independent $\\mathrm{Exp}(0.5)$ component lifetimes (mean 2 yr each). With one component ($\\alpha=1$) the curve is the bare exponential; adding cold spares ($\\alpha=2,3$) pushes the survival curve out and squares off its shoulder — the system reliably reaches its mean life before dropping off. The dot marks the 5-year, $\\alpha=3$ probability computed in the text."
from scipy.stats import gamma as gamma_d
lam = 0.5
t = np.linspace(0, 14, 400)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
for a, c in [(1, GREY), (2, ACCENT), (3, CURVE2)]:
    ax.plot(t, gamma_d.sf(t, a, scale=1/lam), color=c, label=fr"$\alpha={a}$ (mission life)")
ax.plot(5.0, gamma_d.sf(5.0, 3, scale=1/lam), "o", color=CURVE2, ms=7, zorder=5)
ax.set_xlabel("mission duration $t$ (years)"); ax.set_ylabel(r"$P(T>t)$")
ax.set_title("Survival vs. number of redundant components")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — a Bayesian conversion rate (the beta) {#sec-conversion}

A new web design has been shown to $n=`{python} "40"`$ visitors, of whom $k=`{python} "9"`$
converted. The conversion rate $p$ is unknown. Starting from a *uniform* prior
$\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ — "any rate in $[0,1]$ equally plausible" — the posterior after the data
is $\mathrm{Beta}(1+k,\ 1+n-k)=\mathrm{Beta}(10,32)$ by the conjugacy shown above. We read the
posterior off using the beta moments we derived.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-conversion
#| echo: false
from scipy.stats import beta as beta_d
n, k = 40, 9
a_post, b_post = 1 + k, 1 + (n - k)
mean = a_post / (a_post + b_post)
var = a_post*b_post / ((a_post+b_post)**2 * (a_post+b_post+1))
lo, hi = beta_d.ppf([0.025, 0.975], a_post, b_post)
naive = k / n
print(f"posterior = Beta({a_post}, {b_post})")
print(f"posterior mean  = a/(a+b)              = {mean:.4f}")
print(f"naive estimate  = k/n                  = {naive:.4f}")
print(f"posterior sd    = sqrt(var)            = {np.sqrt(var):.4f}")
print(f"95% credible interval                  = [{lo:.3f}, {hi:.3f}]")
```

The posterior mean is $\dfrac{10}{10+32}=`{python} f"{10/42:.4f}"`$ (@thm-beta-moments), a touch
below the naive $k/n=`{python} f"{9/40:.4f}"`$ because the uniform prior gently pulls toward
$1/2$ — the "add one success and one failure" effect of starting from $\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$.
The posterior standard deviation is $`{python} f"{np.sqrt((10*32)/((42**2)*43)):.4f}"`$,
and a $95\%$ credible interval is roughly
$[`{python} f"{beta_d.ppf(0.025,10,32):.3f}"`,\ `{python} f"{beta_d.ppf(0.975,10,32):.3f}"`]$ —
the *whole distribution* of plausible rates, not a point. As more visitors arrive, $\alpha+\beta$
grows and the variance shrinks: the beta tightens around the true rate. @fig-conversion shows
the posterior sharpening with sample size.

```{python}
#| label: fig-conversion
#| fig-cap: "Bayesian updating with the beta. Starting from the flat uniform prior $\\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$ (grey), each curve is the posterior after observing a 22.5\\% conversion rate in $n=10, 40, 200$ visitors. The posterior is always a beta (conjugacy), its mean sits near the observed rate, and it sharpens as $\\alpha+\\beta$ grows — exactly the variance behavior derived in @thm-beta-moments."
from scipy.stats import beta as beta_d
x = np.linspace(0, 1, 500)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
ax.plot(x, beta_d.pdf(x, 1, 1), color=GREY, ls="--", label=r"prior $\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$")
for n, c in [(10, ACCENT), (40, CURVE), (200, CURVE2)]:
    k = round(0.225 * n)
    ax.plot(x, beta_d.pdf(x, 1+k, 1+n-k), color=c,
            label=fr"posterior, $n={n}$ ($k={k}$)")
ax.set_xlabel("conversion rate $p$"); ax.set_ylabel("posterior density")
ax.set_title("Beta posterior sharpens with data")
ax.legend(framealpha=0.9, fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: machine lifetimes that age {#sec-aging}

Now the honest counterexample, the case where the exponential is the *wrong* tool. Pumps,
bearings, and engines do not fail memorylessly — they **wear out**. An old pump is *more*
likely to fail in the next month than a new one, which directly violates @eq-memoryless. If you
model an aging part with an exponential, you will systematically misjudge its risk: too
pessimistic when it is new, dangerously optimistic when it is old.

The right tool is a gamma (or, more commonly in reliability, a Weibull) whose **hazard rate**
*increases* with age. The hazard rate $h(x)=f(x)/P(X>x)$ is the instantaneous failure rate
given survival to $x$; for the exponential it is the *constant* $\lambda$ (that constancy *is*
memorylessness), while for an aging part it must climb. @fig-aging contrasts the two.

```{python}
#| label: fig-aging
#| fig-cap: "Why the exponential is wrong for things that age. **Left:** densities of a memoryless exponential (mean 5 yr) and a gamma with shape $\\alpha=4$ matched to the same mean — the gamma has a hump, peaking at a typical wear-out age, while the exponential insists the most likely lifetime is *zero*. **Right:** the hazard rate $h(x)=f(x)/P(X{>}x)$. The exponential's is flat (constant risk forever — memorylessness); the aging gamma's hazard *rises* with age, the correct shape for wear-out. Fitting an exponential to an aging part assumes the flat line and gets the risk profile qualitatively wrong."
from scipy.stats import expon, gamma as gamma_d
mean = 5.0
x = np.linspace(0.001, 16, 500)
# exponential, mean 5 => lambda = 1/5
f_exp = expon.pdf(x, scale=mean)
s_exp = expon.sf(x, scale=mean)
# gamma shape 4, same mean 5 => scale = mean/alpha = 1.25
a = 4; scale = mean / a
f_gam = gamma_d.pdf(x, a, scale=scale)
s_gam = gamma_d.sf(x, a, scale=scale)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.0, 3.8))
axL.plot(x, f_exp, color=CURVE2, label="exponential (memoryless)")
axL.plot(x, f_gam, color=CURVE, label=r"gamma $\alpha=4$ (ages)")
axL.set_title("Lifetime densities, same mean"); axL.set_xlabel("age (yr)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.plot(x, f_exp/s_exp, color=CURVE2, label="exponential: flat hazard")
axR.plot(x, f_gam/s_gam, color=CURVE, label="gamma: rising hazard")
axR.set_title("Hazard rate $h(x)=f/P(X>x)$"); axR.set_xlabel("age (yr)"); axR.set_ylabel("hazard")
axR.set_ylim(0, 1.0)
axR.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

**When you reach for something else:** if the failure rate changes with age, abandon the
exponential. Use a **gamma** or **Weibull** (the reliability standard, with a tunable
increasing or decreasing hazard), or a **log-normal** for multiplicatively-generated lifetimes.
Reserve the exponential for the genuinely memoryless case — radioactive decay, arrivals in a
stable queue, the gap between independent shocks — where the constant-hazard assumption is
*true*, not merely convenient.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The exponential's defining virtue is its most dangerous assumption.** Memorylessness
(@thm-memoryless) makes the math clean and is the reason the exponential is everywhere in
textbooks — but it asserts that *nothing ages*. Apply it to anything with wear, fatigue,
burn-in, or learning curves and you will get the risk profile qualitatively wrong, as in
@sec-aging. The reflex diagnostic: estimate the hazard rate from your data and see if it is
flat. If it trends up (wear-out) or down (infant mortality), the exponential is the wrong
model and you want a gamma, Weibull, or log-normal.
:::

A few more boundaries worth stating plainly:

- **The gamma's shape $\alpha$ must be estimated, and it is not robust.** Fitting a gamma to
  positive skewed data is reasonable, but the maximum-likelihood estimate of $\alpha$ involves
  the *digamma* function and is sensitive to a few small or zero-valued observations (the
  $x^{\alpha-1}$ factor blows up near $0$ when $\alpha<1$). When the data have exact zeros, the
  gamma is undefined there and you need a zero-inflated or shifted model instead.
- **The beta cannot put mass exactly at $0$ or $1$.** It is a *continuous* density on the open
  interval; $P(X=0)=P(X=1)=0$. If your proportions genuinely pile up at the boundaries — many
  users who convert at rate exactly $0$, say — a plain beta misfits, and you want a
  zero/one-inflated beta or a different model.
- **These are univariate, independent-pieces models.** The gamma-as-sum-of-exponentials story
  (@thm-gamma-sum) and the beta-from-gammas story (@thm-beta-gamma) both *assume independence*.
  Correlated waits (a traffic jam delays every car) or correlated trials break the
  constructions; the marginal may still be gamma or beta, but the sum/ratio identities no
  longer apply.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the exponential the right model, and what is the single assumption you must check?"*
The exponential is right for the **waiting time of a memoryless, constant-rate process** — the
gaps of a Poisson process: radioactive decays, arrivals at a stable queue, independent shocks.
The one assumption to check is **constant hazard** (equivalently, memorylessness): does the
chance of the event in the next instant depend on how long you have already waited? If yes
(aging, wear, fatigue, infant mortality), the exponential is wrong and you escalate to the
gamma or Weibull. State that you would *plot the empirical hazard rate* to decide — that is the
practitioner's move the question is fishing for.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from [PROB-12 *The Poisson Distribution*] — whose event *counts* became the event
*gaps* of this article (@thm-poisson-exp) — and from the continuous-density groundwork of
[PROB-4], [PROB-5], and [PROB-6]. This article built three named continuous distributions end
to end and showed they are one family joined at the gamma function: the exponential
(@sec-exponential), the gamma (@sec-gamma-dist), and the beta (@sec-beta), with the beta–gamma
identity (@thm-beta-gamma) closing the loop. From here the catalog continues and the family's
members fan out:

- **[PROB-15 *Chi-Square, Student's t, and F*]** — the **chi-square** is a special gamma
  ($\mathrm{Gamma}(k/2,\,1/2)$), and the half-integer value $\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt\pi$ we
  flagged in @sec-gamma-function is what normalizes it. Everything classical inference does
  with sample variances runs through that identity.
- **[PROB-16 *The Exponential Family*]** — the exponential, gamma, and beta are all members of
  one canonical algebraic form; this article's densities reappear there as worked examples,
  and the $A'(\theta)=E[T]$ moment trick recovers the means we computed by hand.
- **Across the tracks:** the **beta** and **gamma** are the conjugate priors of
  [INFR-E4] (Beta–Binomial and Gamma–Poisson Bayesian updating), the machinery sketched in
  @sec-conversion; the **exponential/gamma** lifetimes feed survival analysis and reliability
  modeling wherever waiting times appear.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Leonhard Euler introduced the gamma function around 1729 while seeking
  a smooth interpolation of the factorial; the integral form @eq-gamma-function and the name
  $\Gamma$ are due to Legendre. Agner Krarup Erlang's 1909–1917 telephone-traffic papers gave
  the gamma its reliability-and-queueing meaning (the integer-shape gamma is still called the
  *Erlang* distribution); Ernest Rutherford and Hans Geiger's 1908 alpha-particle counts are
  the canonical Poisson-count / exponential-gap dataset.
- **The conjugacy thread.** The beta as the conjugate prior for a probability is the entry
  point to Bayesian inference; its proper home is [INFR-E4], but the algebraic *why* — powers
  of $p$ simply add — is the @sec-conversion calculation. Thomas Bayes' own 1763 essay used,
  in effect, a uniform ($\mathrm{Beta}(1,1)$) prior on a binomial probability.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why $\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt\pi$?* —
  it reduces to the Gaussian integral built in [PROB-13] @sec-gaussian-integral. *What single
  form contains all three of these distributions, the normal, and the Poisson?* — the
  exponential family, [PROB-16]. *How does the chi-square emerge as a gamma, and the t and F
  from it?* — [PROB-15]. *What replaces the exponential when the hazard is not constant?* —
  the Weibull and the broader survival-analysis toolkit, flagged in @sec-aging.
